Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Under IAS 36, when must an entity perform an impairment test for an asset?
options: When there is an indication of impairment, or annually for goodwill and certain intangibles
Only when the asset's market value falls below cost
Never for assets held at historical cost
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment test is required when there are indicators of impairment. In addition, goodwill and indefinite-life intangibles (and certain other intangibles not yet available for use) must be tested for impairment annually.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: What is recoverable amount under IAS 36?
options: The higher of an asset's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Recoverable amount is the higher of an asset's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: What is value in use under IAS 36?
options: The present value of the future cash flows expected to be derived from an asset (or cash-generating unit)
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Value in use is the present value of the future cash flows expected to be derived from an asset or cash-generating unit. It includes estimated future cash inflows and outflows and the appropriate discount rate.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: How is an impairment loss recognised for an individual asset under IAS 36?
options: In profit or loss, and the carrying amount of the asset is reduced (or the related revaluation surplus is reduced first if applicable)
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment loss is recognised in profit or loss. The carrying amount of the asset is reduced. For a revalued asset, the loss is recognised in OCI to the extent of any revaluation surplus for that asset; the remainder in profit or loss.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: What is a cash-generating unit under IAS 36?
options: The smallest identifiable group of assets that generates cash inflows that are largely independent of the cash inflows from other assets or groups of assets
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: A cash-generating unit is the smallest identifiable group of assets that generates cash inflows that are largely independent of the cash inflows from other assets or groups of assets.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: How is goodwill allocated for impairment testing under IAS 36?
options: To the entity as a whole

To each cash-generating unit (or group of units) that is expected to benefit from the synergies of the combination; the allocation is not larger than the level at which goodwill is monitored internally
Goodwill is not tested for impairment
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Goodwill acquired in a business combination is allocated to each of the acquirer's cash-generating units (or groups of units) that are expected to benefit from the synergies. The allocation is at a level not larger than an operating segment.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Can an impairment loss be reversed for goodwill under IAS 36?
options: No; an impairment loss recognised for goodwill is not reversed in a subsequent period
Only if the reason for the loss no longer applies
Only in the year after the loss
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment loss recognised for goodwill is not reversed in a subsequent period. For other assets, reversal is permitted in certain circumstances (recoverable amount increases; reversal is limited).

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: When can an impairment loss (other than for goodwill) be reversed under IAS 36?
options: When there has been a change in the estimates used to determine recoverable amount; the increased carrying amount cannot exceed what the carrying amount would have been (net of depreciation/amortisation) had no impairment been recognised
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: A reversal of an impairment loss (for assets other than goodwill) is recognised when there has been a change in the estimates used. The reversed carrying amount cannot exceed the carrying amount that would have been determined (net of depreciation/amortisation) had no impairment loss been recognised.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: What discount rate should be used in calculating value in use under IAS 36?
options: The entity's weighted average cost of capital
A pre-tax rate that reflects current market assessments of the time value of money and the risks specific to the asset
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: The discount rate used in measuring value in use should be a pre-tax rate that reflects current market assessments of the time value of money and the risks specific to the asset. The entity's WACC may be a starting point but is adjusted for asset-specific risks.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Does IAS 36 apply to inventories, construction contracts, deferred tax assets or assets arising from employee benefits?
options: No; those assets are excluded from the scope of IAS 36
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: IAS 36 does not apply to inventories, construction contract assets, deferred tax assets, assets arising from employee benefits, or financial assets within the scope of IFRS 9, among others.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Exam practice 11: Under IAS 36, when must an entity perform an impairment test for an asset?

This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

For other assets, reversal is permitted in certain circumstances (recoverable amount increases; reversal is limited). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Exam practice 18: When can an impairment loss (other than for goodwill) be reversed under IAS 36?
options: When there has been a change in the estimates used to determine recoverable amount; the increased carrying amount cannot exceed what the carrying amount would have been (net of depreciation/amortisation) had no impairment been recognised
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: A reversal of an impairment loss (for assets other than goodwill) is recognised when there has been a change in the estimates used. The reversed carrying amount cannot exceed the carrying amount that would have been determined (net of depreciation/amortisation) had no impairment loss been recognised. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Exam practice 19: What discount rate should be used in calculating value in use under IAS 36?
options: A pre-tax rate that reflects current market assessments of the time value of money and the risks specific to the asset
The entity's weighted average cost of capital
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: The discount rate used in measuring value in use should be a pre-tax rate that reflects current market assessments of the time value of money and the risks specific to the asset. The entity's WACC may be a starting point but is adjusted for asset-specific risks. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Exam practice 20: Does IAS 36 apply to inventories, construction contracts, deferred tax assets or assets arising from employee benefits?
options: No; those assets are excluded from the scope of IAS 36
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: IAS 36 does not apply to inventories, construction contract assets, deferred tax assets, assets arising from employee benefits, or financial assets within the scope of IFRS 9, among others. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Revision check 21: Under IAS 36, when must an entity perform an impairment test for an asset? Choose the best answer.
options: Only when the asset's market value falls below cost
Never for assets held at historical cost
When there is an indication of impairment, or annually for goodwill and certain intangibles
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment test is required when there are indicators of impairment. In addition, goodwill and indefinite-life intangibles (and certain other intangibles not yet available for use) must be tested for impairment annually. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Revision check 22: What is recoverable amount under IAS 36? Choose the best answer.
options: The higher of an asset's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Recoverable amount is the higher of an asset's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Revision check 23: What is value in use under IAS 36? Choose the best answer.
options: The present value of the future cash flows expected to be derived from an asset (or cash-generating unit)
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Value in use is the present value of the future cash flows expected to be derived from an asset or cash-generating unit. It includes estimated future cash inflows and outflows and the appropriate discount rate. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Revision check 24: How is an impairment loss recognised for an individual asset under IAS 36? Choose the best answer.
options: In profit or loss, and the carrying amount of the asset is reduced (or the related revaluation surplus is reduced first if applicable)
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment loss is recognised in profit or loss. The carrying amount of the asset is reduced. For a revalued asset, the loss is recognised in OCI to the extent of any revaluation surplus for that asset; the remainder in profit or loss. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Revision check 25: What is a cash-generating unit under IAS 36? Choose the best answer.
options: The smallest identifiable group of assets that generates cash inflows that are largely independent of the cash inflows from other assets or groups of assets
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: A cash-generating unit is the smallest identifiable group of assets that generates cash inflows that are largely independent of the cash inflows from other assets or groups of assets. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Revision check 26: How is goodwill allocated for impairment testing under IAS 36? Choose the best answer.
options: Goodwill is not tested for impairment
To the entity as a whole

To each cash-generating unit (or group of units) that is expected to benefit from the synergies of the combination; the allocation is not larger than the level at which goodwill is monitored internally
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Goodwill acquired in a business combination is allocated to each of the acquirer's cash-generating units (or groups of units) that are expected to benefit from the synergies. The allocation is at a level not larger than an operating segment. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Revision check 27: Can an impairment loss be reversed for goodwill under IAS 36? Choose the best answer.
options: Only if the reason for the loss no longer applies
Only in the year after the loss
No; an impairment loss recognised for goodwill is not reversed in a subsequent period
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment loss recognised for goodwill is not reversed in a subsequent period. For other assets, reversal is permitted in certain circumstances (recoverable amount increases; reversal is limited). This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: Under IFRS 16, how does a lessee initially recognise a lease?
options: Only as an expense in profit or loss
As a right-of-use asset and a lease liability
As a finance lease asset and obligation only
Off balance sheet with disclosure only
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: IFRS 16 requires lessees to recognise a right-of-use asset and a lease liability at the commencement date for most leases (with limited exceptions for short-term and low-value leases).

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: What is the lease term under IFRS 16?
options: The non-cancellable period plus any extension options the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise, and any period covered by a termination option the lessee is reasonably certain not to exercise
The period until the first break clause
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: The lease term includes the non-cancellable period, plus periods covered by extension options if the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise, and excludes periods covered by termination options if the lessee is reasonably certain not to exercise.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: How is the lease liability initially measured under IFRS 16?
options: At the sum of undiscounted lease payments
At the present value of the lease payments that are not paid at the commencement date, discounted using the interest rate implicit in the lease or the lessee's incremental borrowing rate
At the minimum lease payments only
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: The lease liability is measured at the present value of the lease payments that are not paid at the commencement date. The discount rate is the rate implicit in the lease, or if not readily determinable, the lessee's incremental borrowing rate.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: What are the optional exemptions for lessees under IFRS 16?
options: Short-term leases (12 months or less) and leases of low-value assets may be accounted for by recognising lease payments as an expense
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: A lessee may elect not to apply the right-of-use asset and lease liability model to short-term leases (lease term of 12 months or less) and to leases of low-value assets (e.g. personal computers). In those cases, lease payments are recognised as an expense.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: How is the right-of-use asset initially measured under IFRS 16?
options: At the lease liability amount only

At the amount of the initial lease liability, plus any initial direct costs, plus any payments at or before commencement (less any lease incentives), and any estimated costs of dismantling or restoring the underlying asset
At the present value of minimum lease payments only
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: The right-of-use asset is initially measured at the amount of the lease liability, plus initial direct costs, plus payments at or before commencement (less incentives), and (if applicable) estimated costs of dismantling/restoring the underlying asset.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: After initial recognition, how is the lease liability measured under IFRS 16?
options: At amortised cost using the effective interest method
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: The lease liability is subsequently measured at amortised cost using the effective interest method. It is increased by the interest charge and reduced by the lease payments.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: How does a lessor classify a lease under IFRS 16?
options: As either a finance lease or an operating lease
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: Lessors classify each lease as either an operating lease or a finance lease. A lease is a finance lease if it transfers substantially all the risks and rewards incidental to ownership of the underlying asset.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: How does a lessor account for a finance lease under IFRS 16?
options: By recognising a lease receivable (net investment in the lease) and derecognising the underlying asset; interest income is recognised over the lease term
By recognising lease payments as income when received
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: A lessor recognises assets held under a finance lease as a receivable at an amount equal to the net investment in the lease. The underlying asset is derecognised. Finance income is allocated to give a constant periodic return on the net investment.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: How does a lessor account for an operating lease under IFRS 16?
options: The underlying asset is presented in the statement of financial position; lease income is recognised on a straight-line or other systematic basis over the lease term
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: For an operating lease, the lessor continues to present the underlying asset in the statement of financial position. Lease income is recognised on a straight-line or other systematic basis over the lease term.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.question: What is the definition of a lease under IFRS 16?
options: Any contract that mentions an asset
A contract, or part of a contract, that conveys the right to use an asset for a period of time in exchange for consideration
Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: A lease is a contract, or part of a contract, that conveys the right to use an asset (the underlying asset) for a period of time in exchange for consideration. A contract is or contains a lease if it conveys the right to control the use of an identified asset for a period of time in exchange for consideration.

Chapter 14: IFRS 16 Leases.explanation: Lessors classify each lease as either an operating lease or a finance lease. A lease is a finance lease if it transfers substantially all the risks and rewards incidental to ownership of the underlying asset. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: Under IAS 37, when should a provision be recognised?
options: When an outflow of resources is possible
When an outflow of resources is certain
When there is a present obligation, probable outflow and reliable estimate
Whenever a legal claim is made
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: A provision is recognised when the entity has a present obligation (legal or constructive) as a result of a past event, it is probable that an outflow of resources will be required, and a reliable estimate can be made.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: What is a constructive obligation under IAS 37?
options: An obligation that arises from the entity's actions (e.g. past practice or published policy) that has created a valid expectation in other parties that the entity will discharge the obligation
An obligation that is not enforceable
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: A constructive obligation arises when the entity has indicated to other parties that it will accept certain responsibilities, and as a result has created a valid expectation that it will discharge those responsibilities.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: How should a provision be measured under IAS 37?
options: At the best estimate of the expenditure required to settle the present obligation at the reporting date
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: A provision is measured at the best estimate of the expenditure required to settle the present obligation at the end of the reporting period. Where the provision relates to a large population of items, expected value may be used.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: When should discounting be applied to a provision under IAS 37?
options: When the effect of the time value of money is material
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: Where the effect of the time value of money is material, the amount of a provision is the present value of the expenditures expected to be required to settle the obligation.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: What is a contingent liability under IAS 37?
options: A possible obligation arising from past events whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non-occurrence of one or more uncertain future events; or a present obligation that is not recognised (e.g. because outflow is not probable or cannot be measured reliably)
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: A contingent liability is either (a) a possible obligation arising from past events to be confirmed by future events, or (b) a present obligation that is not recognised because outflow is not probable or the amount cannot be estimated reliably.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: How should a contingent liability be accounted for under IAS 37?
options: It is not recognised as a provision; it is disclosed unless the possibility of outflow is remote
As a provision at nominal amount
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: Contingent liabilities are not recognised as provisions. They are disclosed in the notes unless the possibility of an outflow of resources is remote.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: What is a contingent asset under IAS 37?
options: A possible asset arising from past events whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non-occurrence of one or more uncertain future events
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: A contingent asset is a possible asset arising from past events whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non-occurrence of one or more uncertain future events not wholly within the entity's control.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: When may a contingent asset be recognised under IAS 37?
options: When the inflow of economic benefits is virtually certain; until then it is not recognised but may be disclosed
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: Contingent assets are not recognised. When the realisation of income is virtually certain, the related asset is not a contingent asset and its recognition is appropriate. Otherwise, disclosure may be made if the inflow is probable.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: How are reimbursements (e.g. from insurers) relating to a provision treated under IAS 37?
options: The reimbursement is recognised as a separate asset when it is virtually certain that it will be received; the expense may be presented net of the reimbursement
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: Where some or all of the expenditure required to settle a provision is expected to be reimbursed (e.g. by an insurer), the reimbursement is recognised as a separate asset when it is virtually certain. The expense may be presented net of the reimbursement in the statement of profit or loss.

Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.question: What types of provisions are specifically addressed in IAS 37?
options: Onerous contracts, restructuring, and others; the standard gives guidance on application
Chapter 15: IAS 37 Provisions, Contingen.explanation: IAS 37 gives specific guidance on provisions for restructurings, onerous contracts and other areas. The general recognition and measurement principles apply to all provisions within scope.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Under IAS 10, how should adjusting events after the reporting period be reflected?
options: In the next period's financial statements only
By amending the amounts recognised in the financial statements
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Adjusting events after the reporting period provide evidence of conditions that existed at the end of the reporting period. They require adjustment of the amounts recognised in the financial statements.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: What is an adjusting event under IAS 10?
options: Any event after the reporting period
An event after the reporting period that provides evidence of conditions that existed at the end of the reporting period
An event that occurs in the next reporting period
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Adjusting events are those that provide evidence of conditions that existed at the end of the reporting period. They require adjustment of the amounts recognised in the financial statements.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: What is a non-adjusting event under IAS 10?
options: An event that always requires adjustment
An event after the reporting period that is indicative of conditions that arose after the reporting period
An event before the reporting period
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Non-adjusting events are those that are indicative of conditions that arose after the reporting period. They do not result in adjustment of the amounts in the financial statements but may require disclosure.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: When must an entity not prepare financial statements on a going concern basis under IAS 10?
options: When events after the reporting period indicate that the entity is not a going concern
Never; going concern is always assumed
Only when the entity is insolvent
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: If management determines after the reporting period that the entity is not a going concern, the financial statements are not prepared on a going concern basis. IAS 10 requires consideration of such events.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: How should dividends declared after the reporting period be treated under IAS 10?
options: As a liability at the reporting date

Dividends declared after the reporting period are not recognised as a liability at the reporting date; they are disclosed if they are of such importance that non-disclosure would affect the ability of users to make proper decisions
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Dividends declared after the reporting period (to holders of equity instruments) are not recognised as a liability at the reporting date. They may be disclosed in the notes. Dividends that are declared from pre-reporting-date profits by the reporting date may be recognised if the entity has an obligation at the reporting date.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Can a decline in market value of investments after the reporting period be an adjusting event?
options: Generally no; it usually reflects conditions that arose after the reporting period (e.g. new information). Bankruptcy of an investee that existed at the reporting date could be adjusting
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: A decline in market value after the reporting period normally reflects new information and is non-adjusting. If it provides evidence of a condition that existed at the end of the reporting period (e.g. bankruptcy of an investee), it may be adjusting.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: What is the role of the date when the financial statements are authorised for issue?
options: The entity does not adjust the amounts in the financial statements for events after the reporting period that are reported to the entity after the authorisation date; disclosure may be required
All events up to that date are adjusting
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: If events after the reporting period that are reported to the entity after the financial statements are authorised for issue would have been adjusted for had they been known, the entity does not adjust the amounts but may need to disclose the event and its impact.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Is the discovery of fraud or an error that occurred before the reporting period an adjusting or non-adjusting event?
options: Adjusting; it provides evidence of a condition that existed at the reporting date
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: The discovery of fraud or an error that shows the financial statements were misstated at the reporting date is an adjusting event because it provides evidence of conditions that existed at the end of the reporting period.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: How should an entity disclose non-adjusting events under IAS 10?
options: The nature of the event and an estimate of its financial effect (or a statement that the estimate cannot be made) are disclosed
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: For material non-adjusting events, the entity discloses the nature of the event and an estimate of its financial effect, or a statement that such an estimate cannot be made.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Does IAS 10 define the date of authorisation for issue?
options: Yes; it is the date when the financial statements are authorised for issue (e.g. by the board or equivalent). The date is disclosed
It is the audit report date
It is the reporting period end
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: The date of authorisation for issue is when the financial statements are authorised for issue (e.g. by the board). IAS 10 requires disclosure of this date and of who gave the authorisation.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Non-adjusting events are those that are indicative of conditions that arose after the reporting period. They do not result in adjustment of the amounts in the financial statements but may require disclosure. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Revision check 24: When must an entity not prepare financial statements on a going concern basis under IAS 10? Choose the best answer.
options: Never; going concern is always assumed
Only when the entity is insolvent
When events after the reporting period indicate that the entity is not a going concern
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: If management determines after the reporting period that the entity is not a going concern, the financial statements are not prepared on a going concern basis. IAS 10 requires consideration of such events. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Revision check 25: How should dividends declared after the reporting period be treated under IAS 10? Choose the best answer.
options: As a liability at the reporting date

Dividends declared after the reporting period are not recognised as a liability at the reporting date; they are disclosed if they are of such importance that non-disclosure would affect the ability of users to make proper decisions
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Dividends declared after the reporting period (to holders of equity instruments) are not recognised as a liability at the reporting date. They may be disclosed in the notes. Dividends that are declared from pre-reporting-date profits by the reporting date may be recognised if the entity has an obligation at the reporting date. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Revision check 26: Can a decline in market value of investments after the reporting period be an adjusting event? Choose the best answer.
options: Generally no; it usually reflects conditions that arose after the reporting period (e.g. new information). Bankruptcy of an investee that existed at the reporting date could be adjusting
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: A decline in market value after the reporting period normally reflects new information and is non-adjusting. If it provides evidence of a condition that existed at the end of the reporting period (e.g. bankruptcy of an investee), it may be adjusting. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Revision check 27: What is the role of the date when the financial statements are authorised for issue? Choose the best answer.
options: All events up to that date are adjusting
The entity does not adjust the amounts in the financial statements for events after the reporting period that are reported to the entity after the authorisation date; disclosure may be required
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: If events after the reporting period that are reported to the entity after the financial statements are authorised for issue would have been adjusted for had they been known, the entity does not adjust the amounts but may need to disclose the event and its impact. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Revision check 28: Is the discovery of fraud or an error that occurred before the reporting period an adjusting or non-adjusting event? Choose the best answer.
options: Adjusting; it provides evidence of a condition that existed at the reporting date
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: The discovery of fraud or an error that shows the financial statements were misstated at the reporting date is an adjusting event because it provides evidence of conditions that existed at the end of the reporting period. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Revision check 29: How should an entity disclose non-adjusting events under IAS 10? Choose the best answer.
options: The nature of the event and an estimate of its financial effect (or a statement that the estimate cannot be made) are disclosed
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: For material non-adjusting events, the entity discloses the nature of the event and an estimate of its financial effect, or a statement that such an estimate cannot be made. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: Revision check 30: Does IAS 10 define the date of authorisation for issue? Choose the best answer.
options: It is the audit report date
It is the reporting period end
Yes; it is the date when the financial statements are authorised for issue (e.g. by the board or equivalent). The date is disclosed
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: The date of authorisation for issue is when the financial statements are authorised for issue (e.g. by the board). IAS 10 requires disclosure of this date and of who gave the authorisation. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: FR quick drill 31: Under IAS 10, how should adjusting events after the reporting period be reflected?
options: In the next period's financial statements only
By amending the amounts recognised in the financial statements
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Adjusting events after the reporting period provide evidence of conditions that existed at the end of the reporting period. They require adjustment of the amounts recognised in the financial statements. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: FR quick drill 32: What is an adjusting event under IAS 10?
options: Any event after the reporting period
An event after the reporting period that provides evidence of conditions that existed at the end of the reporting period
An event that occurs in the next reporting period
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Adjusting events are those that provide evidence of conditions that existed at the end of the reporting period. They require adjustment of the amounts recognised in the financial statements. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: FR quick drill 33: What is a non-adjusting event under IAS 10?
options: An event that always requires adjustment
An event after the reporting period that is indicative of conditions that arose after the reporting period
An event before the reporting period
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Non-adjusting events are those that are indicative of conditions that arose after the reporting period. They do not result in adjustment of the amounts in the financial statements but may require disclosure. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: FR quick drill 34: When must an entity not prepare financial statements on a going concern basis under IAS 10?
options: Only when the entity is insolvent
When events after the reporting period indicate that the entity is not a going concern
Never; going concern is always assumed
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: If management determines after the reporting period that the entity is not a going concern, the financial statements are not prepared on a going concern basis. IAS 10 requires consideration of such events. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: FR quick drill 35: How should dividends declared after the reporting period be treated under IAS 10?
options: As a liability at the reporting date

Dividends declared after the reporting period are not recognised as a liability at the reporting date; they are disclosed if they are of such importance that non-disclosure would affect the ability of users to make proper decisions
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: Dividends declared after the reporting period (to holders of equity instruments) are not recognised as a liability at the reporting date. They may be disclosed in the notes. Dividends that are declared from pre-reporting-date profits by the reporting date may be recognised if the entity has an obligation at the reporting date. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: FR quick drill 36: Can a decline in market value of investments after the reporting period be an adjusting event?
options: Generally no; it usually reflects conditions that arose after the reporting period (e.g. new information). Bankruptcy of an investee that existed at the reporting date could be adjusting
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: A decline in market value after the reporting period normally reflects new information and is non-adjusting. If it provides evidence of a condition that existed at the end of the reporting period (e.g. bankruptcy of an investee), it may be adjusting. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.question: FR quick drill 37: What is the role of the date when the financial statements are authorised for issue?
options: The entity does not adjust the amounts in the financial statements for events after the reporting period that are reported to the entity after the authorisation date; disclosure may be required
All events up to that date are adjusting
Chapter 16: IAS 10 Events after the Repo.explanation: If events after the reporting period that are reported to the entity after the financial statements are authorised for issue would have been adjusted for had they been known, the entity does not adjust the amounts but may need to disclose the event and its impact. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Under IAS 12, what is a deferred tax liability?
options: Tax payable in the current period
The amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences
An estimate of future tax refunds
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax liability is the amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences. Taxable temporary differences result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: What is a deferred tax asset under IAS 12?
options: The amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, unused tax losses and unused tax credits
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax asset is the amount of income tax recoverable in future periods in respect of deductible temporary differences, carryforward of unused tax losses and unused tax credits.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: What is a taxable temporary difference?
options: A difference that gives rise to a deferred tax asset
A difference that will result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Taxable temporary differences are temporary differences that will result in taxable amounts in determining taxable profit (tax loss) of future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: At what rate should deferred tax be measured under IAS 12?
options: At the current tax rate only

At the tax rates that are expected to apply to the period when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, based on tax rates (and laws) that have been enacted or substantively enacted by the end of the reporting period
At the average rate of the last 5 years
At the rate applicable when the temporary difference originated
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Deferred tax is measured at the tax rates that are expected to apply when the asset is realised or the liability is settled, using tax rates (and laws) enacted or substantively enacted at the reporting date.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: When may a deferred tax asset be recognised under IAS 12?
options: Always when there is a deductible temporary difference
To the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which the deductible temporary difference, loss or credit can be utilised
Only when the loss is used
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax asset is recognised for deductible temporary differences, unused losses and unused tax credits to the extent that it is probable that taxable profit will be available against which they can be utilised.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: How is current tax defined in IAS 12?
options: The amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past periods
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Current tax is the amount of income taxes payable (recoverable) in respect of the taxable profit (tax loss) for the current or past reporting periods.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Where is current tax and deferred tax presented in the financial statements under IAS 12?
options: As income tax expense (or income) in profit or loss, except to the extent that it relates to items recognised in OCI or directly in equity
Only in the statement of financial position
As a separate line in equity
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: Current and deferred tax are recognised as income or expense and included in profit or loss for the period, except to the extent that the tax arises from (a) a transaction or event recognised in OCI or directly in equity, or (b) a business combination.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Can deferred tax arise from revaluation of assets under IAS 12?
options: Yes; if the carrying amount is revalued (e.g. under IAS 16), the tax base is not adjusted, so a temporary difference arises and deferred tax is recognised (often in OCI)
Only when the asset is sold
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: When an asset is revalued, its carrying amount changes but its tax base generally does not, so a temporary difference arises. Deferred tax on revaluations may be recognised in OCI to the extent the revaluation is in OCI.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: What is the tax base of an asset under IAS 12?
options: The amount attributable to the asset for tax purposes
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: The tax base of an asset is the amount that would be deductible for tax purposes against any taxable economic benefits that would flow to the entity on recovery of the carrying amount. If those benefits are not taxable, the tax base equals the carrying amount.

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Does IAS 12 require disclosure of a reconciliation of tax expense?
options: Yes; a numerical reconciliation between tax expense (income) and the product of accounting profit multiplied by the applicable tax rate(s), or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate
Only when the effective rate differs from the statutory rate by more than 5%
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: IAS 12 requires an explanation of the relationship between tax expense (income) and accounting profit (e.g. a numerical reconciliation between tax expense and the product of accounting profit and the applicable tax rate, or a reconciliation of the average effective tax rate).

Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.question: Exam practice 11: Under IAS 12, what is a deferred tax liability?
options: The amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences
An estimate of future tax refunds
Tax payable in the current period
Chapter 17: IAS 12 Income Taxes.explanation: A deferred tax liability is the amount of income tax payable in future periods in respect of taxable temporary differences. Taxable temporary differences result in taxable amounts in future periods when the carrying amount of the asset or liability is recovered or settled. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Under IFRS 9, how are debt instruments measured when held in the amortised cost category?
options: At fair value through profit or loss
At amortised cost using the effective interest method
At fair value through other comprehensive income only
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Financial assets measured at amortised cost are initially recognised at fair value and subsequently at amortised cost using the effective interest method, less any impairment. The business model must be to hold to collect contractual cash flows.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: What are the three main classification categories for financial assets under IFRS 9?
options: Held to maturity, available for sale, loans and receivables
Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Under IFRS 9, financial assets are classified as measured at amortised cost, at fair value through other comprehensive income, or at fair value through profit or loss, depending on the business model and the contractual cash flow characteristics.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: When can a debt instrument be measured at amortised cost under IFRS 9?
options: Whenever the entity intends to hold it

When the asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and the contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding
Only if it is not quoted
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial asset is measured at amortised cost if (1) it is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and (2) its contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest (SPPI).

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: What is the effective interest method under IFRS 9?
options: A method of allocating interest income or expense at a constant rate over the relevant period

A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: The effective interest method is a method of calculating the amortised cost of a financial asset or liability and of allocating the interest income or expense over the relevant period. The effective interest rate is the rate that exactly discounts estimated future cash flows to the instrument's carrying amount.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: How is impairment of financial assets at amortised cost measured under IFRS 9?
options: Only when the asset is past due
Using an expected credit loss (ECL) model; loss allowances are recognised for 12-month ECL or lifetime ECL depending on whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk
At the amount of principal outstanding
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: IFRS 9 requires an expected credit loss model. For assets that have not had a significant increase in credit risk since initial recognition, 12-month ECL is recognised. For those that have, lifetime ECL is recognised.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: When is a financial liability measured at amortised cost under IFRS 9?
options: Generally, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Financial liabilities are generally measured at amortised cost using the effective interest method, except for those designated at fair value through profit or loss (e.g. to eliminate an accounting mismatch).

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: What is a financial instrument under IFRS 9?
options: A contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial instrument is any contract that gives rise to a financial asset of one entity and a financial liability or equity instrument of another entity. It includes primary instruments (e.g. receivables, debt) and derivatives.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Can an entity designate a financial asset at fair value through profit or loss to eliminate an accounting mismatch?
options: Yes; in limited circumstances, an entity may designate a financial asset (or group) at fair value through profit or loss if doing so eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: An entity may designate a financial asset at fair value through profit or loss at initial recognition if this eliminates or significantly reduces a measurement or recognition inconsistency (an 'accounting mismatch') that would otherwise arise from measuring assets and liabilities on different bases.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: How are equity instruments (e.g. shares in other entities) generally measured under IFRS 9?
options: At fair value through profit or loss, unless the entity makes an irrevocable election to present changes in fair value in OCI (with no recycling)
At the lower of cost and fair value
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Investments in equity instruments are generally measured at fair value through profit or loss. An irrevocable election may be made at initial recognition to present changes in fair value in other comprehensive income (with no recycling to profit or loss on derecognition).

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: When is a financial asset derecognised under IFRS 9?
options: When the contractual rights to the cash flows from the asset expire, or the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. transfer of substantially all risks and rewards)
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial asset is derecognised when (a) the contractual rights to the cash flows expire, or (b) the entity transfers the asset and the transfer qualifies for derecognition (e.g. the entity has transferred substantially all risks and rewards of ownership).

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 11: Under IFRS 9, how are debt instruments measured when held in the amortised cost category?
options: At amortised cost using the effective interest method
At fair value through other comprehensive income only
At fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Financial assets measured at amortised cost are initially recognised at fair value and subsequently at amortised cost using the effective interest method, less any impairment. The business model must be to hold to collect contractual cash flows. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 12: What are the three main classification categories for financial assets under IFRS 9?
options: Amortised cost, fair value through other comprehensive income, and fair value through profit or loss
Held to maturity, available for sale, loans and receivables
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: Under IFRS 9, financial assets are classified as measured at amortised cost, at fair value through other comprehensive income, or at fair value through profit or loss, depending on the business model and the contractual cash flow characteristics. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 13: When can a debt instrument be measured at amortised cost under IFRS 9?
options: When the asset is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and the contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest on the principal amount outstanding
Only if it is not quoted
Whenever the entity intends to hold it
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: A financial asset is measured at amortised cost if (1) it is held within a business model whose objective is to hold assets to collect contractual cash flows, and (2) its contractual terms give rise to cash flows that are solely payments of principal and interest (SPPI). This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.question: Exam practice 14: What is the effective interest method under IFRS 9?
options: A method of allocating the interest income/expense (and fees, transaction costs, premiums, discounts) over the expected life of the instrument so as to produce a constant periodic rate of interest on the carrying amount
A method of allocating interest income or expense at a constant rate over the relevant period
Chapter 18: Financial Instruments.explanation: The effective interest method is a method of calculating the amortised cost of a financial asset or liability and of allocating the interest income or expense over the relevant period. The effective interest rate is the rate that exactly discounts estimated future cash flows to the instrument's carrying amount. This is variant 2 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Under IAS 21, at what exchange rate should a foreign currency transaction be initially recorded?
options: The rate at the year end
The average rate for the period
The spot rate at the date of the transaction
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: IAS 21 requires a foreign currency transaction to be recorded, on initial recognition, in the functional currency by applying to the foreign currency amount the spot exchange rate between the functional currency and the foreign currency at the date of the transaction.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: What is the functional currency under IAS 21?
options: The currency of the country where the entity is registered
The currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates
The currency in which the entity presents its financial statements
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: The functional currency is the currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates (i.e. the environment in which it generates and spends cash).

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: What is the presentation currency under IAS 21?
options: The currency in which the financial statements are presented
The currency of the country of listing
The currency of the parent only
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: The presentation currency is the currency in which the financial statements are presented. It may be different from the functional currency.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: At what rate should foreign currency monetary items be translated at the reporting date under IAS 21?
options: At the rate at the transaction date
At the closing rate (spot rate at the end of the reporting period)
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: At the end of each reporting period, foreign currency monetary items are translated using the closing rate. Non-monetary items carried at historical cost are translated at the transaction date rate.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Where are foreign exchange differences on monetary items recognised under IAS 21?
options: In profit or loss (except when the gain or loss is part of the net investment in a foreign operation and the item is accounted for as equity)
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Exchange differences arising on the translation of monetary items (or on the settlement of monetary items) are generally recognised in profit or loss in the period in which they arise. Exceptions apply for certain net investment in a foreign operation and for hedge accounting.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: How are non-monetary items carried at fair value in a foreign currency translated under IAS 21?
options: At the rate at the date when the fair value was determined
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: A non-monetary item measured at fair value in a foreign currency is translated using the exchange rate at the date when the fair value was determined. This produces the carrying amount in the functional currency.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: How should the results and financial position of a foreign operation be translated for inclusion in the entity's financial statements under IAS 21?
options: Assets and liabilities at the closing rate; income and expenses at the exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate); equity at historical rates
All at the transaction date rate
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Assets and liabilities of the foreign operation are translated at the closing rate. Income and expenses are translated at exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate). Equity components are translated at historical rates. Exchange differences are recognised in OCI.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Where are exchange differences arising on the translation of a foreign operation recognised when the operation is consolidated?
options: In other comprehensive income (and accumulated in a separate component of equity) until disposal of the net investment
As a reserve that is recycled to profit or loss
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Exchange differences resulting from translating the results and financial position of a foreign operation (e.g. subsidiary) into the presentation currency of the reporting entity are recognised in other comprehensive income and accumulated in equity (foreign currency translation reserve) until disposal of the net investment.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: When an entity's functional currency is not the currency of a hyperinflationary economy but the presentation currency is, how are the financial statements translated?
options: The financial statements are first restated in accordance with IAS 29 (if the functional currency is hyperinflationary), then translated using the closing rate
Only the balance sheet is translated
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: When the presentation currency is the currency of a hyperinflationary economy, the entity restates its financial statements in accordance with IAS 29 before translation. When the functional currency is hyperinflationary, the entity applies IAS 29 and then translates using the closing rate.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Can the functional currency of an entity change under IAS 21?
options: Yes; if there is a change in the underlying transactions, events and conditions, the functional currency may change. The change is applied prospectively
Only when the entity moves country
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: If the underlying transactions, events and conditions change, the functional currency may change. The entity applies the new functional currency prospectively from the date of the change.

Assets and liabilities at the closing rate; income and expenses at the exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate); equity at historical rates
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Assets and liabilities of the foreign operation are translated at the closing rate. Income and expenses are translated at exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate). Equity components are translated at historical rates. Exchange differences are recognised in OCI. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Revision check 28: Where are exchange differences arising on the translation of a foreign operation recognised when the operation is consolidated? Choose the best answer.
options: As a reserve that is recycled to profit or loss
In other comprehensive income (and accumulated in a separate component of equity) until disposal of the net investment
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Exchange differences resulting from translating the results and financial position of a foreign operation (e.g. subsidiary) into the presentation currency of the reporting entity are recognised in other comprehensive income and accumulated in equity (foreign currency translation reserve) until disposal of the net investment. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Revision check 29: When an entity's functional currency is not the currency of a hyperinflationary economy but the presentation currency is, how are the financial statements translated? Choose the best answer.
options: Only the balance sheet is translated
The financial statements are first restated in accordance with IAS 29 (if the functional currency is hyperinflationary), then translated using the closing rate
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: When the presentation currency is the currency of a hyperinflationary economy, the entity restates its financial statements in accordance with IAS 29 before translation. When the functional currency is hyperinflationary, the entity applies IAS 29 and then translates using the closing rate. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: Revision check 30: Can the functional currency of an entity change under IAS 21? Choose the best answer.
options: Only when the entity moves country
Yes; if there is a change in the underlying transactions, events and conditions, the functional currency may change. The change is applied prospectively
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: If the underlying transactions, events and conditions change, the functional currency may change. The entity applies the new functional currency prospectively from the date of the change. This is variant 3 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: FR quick drill 31: Under IAS 21, at what exchange rate should a foreign currency transaction be initially recorded?
options: The rate at the year end
The average rate for the period
The spot rate at the date of the transaction
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: IAS 21 requires a foreign currency transaction to be recorded, on initial recognition, in the functional currency by applying to the foreign currency amount the spot exchange rate between the functional currency and the foreign currency at the date of the transaction. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: FR quick drill 32: What is the functional currency under IAS 21?
options: The currency of the country where the entity is registered
The currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates
The currency in which the entity presents its financial statements
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: The functional currency is the currency of the primary economic environment in which the entity operates (i.e. the environment in which it generates and spends cash). This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: FR quick drill 33: What is the presentation currency under IAS 21?
options: The currency of the parent only
The currency in which the financial statements are presented
The currency of the country of listing
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: The presentation currency is the currency in which the financial statements are presented. It may be different from the functional currency. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: FR quick drill 34: At what rate should foreign currency monetary items be translated at the reporting date under IAS 21?
options: At the rate at the transaction date
At the closing rate (spot rate at the end of the reporting period)
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: At the end of each reporting period, foreign currency monetary items are translated using the closing rate. Non-monetary items carried at historical cost are translated at the transaction date rate. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: FR quick drill 35: Where are foreign exchange differences on monetary items recognised under IAS 21?
options: In profit or loss (except when the gain or loss is part of the net investment in a foreign operation and the item is accounted for as equity)
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Exchange differences arising on the translation of monetary items (or on the settlement of monetary items) are generally recognised in profit or loss in the period in which they arise. Exceptions apply for certain net investment in a foreign operation and for hedge accounting. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: FR quick drill 36: How are non-monetary items carried at fair value in a foreign currency translated under IAS 21?
options: At the rate at the date when the fair value was determined
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: A non-monetary item measured at fair value in a foreign currency is translated using the exchange rate at the date when the fair value was determined. This produces the carrying amount in the functional currency. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.question: FR quick drill 37: How should the results and financial position of a foreign operation be translated for inclusion in the entity's financial statements under IAS 21?
options: All at the transaction date rate
Assets and liabilities at the closing rate; income and expenses at the exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate); equity at historical rates
Chapter 19: Foreign Currency Transaction.explanation: Assets and liabilities of the foreign operation are translated at the closing rate. Income and expenses are translated at exchange rates at the dates of the transactions (or an average rate). Equity components are translated at historical rates. Exchange differences are recognised in OCI. This is variant 4 of the same tested FR concept.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Under IAS 33, how is basic earnings per share calculated?
options: Profit divided by weighted average number of ordinary shares
Profit after tax divided by number of shares at year end
Operating profit divided by issued shares
Profit divided by number of shares at the start of the period
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Basic EPS = (Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders) / (Weighted average number of ordinary shares outstanding during the period).

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: What is the numerator (profit) for basic EPS under IAS 33?
options: Profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent entity (after deducting preference dividends and other adjustments for preferred equity)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: The numerator is profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent. It is after tax and after deducting preference dividends (and other appropriations in respect of preference shares) for the period.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: How is the weighted average number of ordinary shares calculated when new shares are issued for cash during the period?
options: The number of ordinary shares outstanding at the beginning, adjusted by the number of shares issued or bought back, weighted by the proportion of the period they were outstanding
Simple average of opening and closing
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: The weighted average number of ordinary shares during the period is the number of shares outstanding at the beginning of the period, adjusted by shares issued or cancelled during the period, weighted by the number of days they were outstanding (or a reasonable approximation).

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: What is a dilutive potential ordinary share under IAS 33?
options: A potential ordinary share whose conversion or exercise would decrease earnings per share (or increase loss per share)
A share option that is out of the money
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: A dilutive potential ordinary share is one that would decrease profit per share (or increase loss per share) from continuing operations. Only dilutive instruments are included in diluted EPS.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: How is diluted EPS calculated under IAS 33?
options: Basic EPS adjusted for all potential ordinary shares

Profit attributable to ordinary equity holders plus the after-tax effect of dilutive potential ordinary shares, divided by the weighted average number of ordinary shares plus the weighted average number of shares that would be issued on conversion/exercise of dilutive potential ordinary shares
Basic EPS minus the effect of options
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Diluted EPS adjusts the numerator for the after-tax effect of dilutive potential ordinary shares and the denominator for the number of additional ordinary shares that would be issued if dilutive instruments were converted or exercised.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: When are contingently issuable shares included in diluted EPS?
options: If the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the period (the shares are included from the beginning of the period or from the date the contingency was agreed)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Contingently issuable ordinary shares are included in diluted EPS from the beginning of the period (or from the date of the contingent share agreement) if the conditions have been satisfied by the end of the reporting period.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: How are share options and warrants typically reflected in diluted EPS?
options: By adding the number of options to the denominator
Using the treasury share method: assume proceeds from exercise are used to buy back ordinary shares at the average market price; the net increase in shares is added to the denominator
By deducting the option premium from profit
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: The treasury share method assumes that the proceeds from exercise of options/warrants are used to buy back ordinary shares at the average market price during the period. The net increase in the number of shares (shares that would be issued less shares deemed repurchased) is dilutive and is added to the denominator.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: Does IAS 33 require disclosure of basic and diluted EPS for profit from continuing operations?
options: Yes; an entity presents basic and diluted EPS for profit or loss from continuing operations and for profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: IAS 33 requires presentation of basic and diluted EPS for profit or loss from continuing operations and for profit or loss attributable to ordinary equity holders of the parent. EPS may also be presented for other line items (e.g. discontinued operations).

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: What is an ordinary share under IAS 33?
options: An equity instrument that is subordinate to all other classes of equity instruments (i.e. carries the residual interest)
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: Ordinary shares are equity instruments that are subordinate to all other classes of equity instruments. They participate in profit for the period only after other types of shares (e.g. preference shares) have been allocated.

Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.question: When comparative figures are restated (e.g. for a prior period error), how should EPS be presented?
options: Basic and diluted EPS for the current period and for any comparative periods presented are restated
Chapter 20: IAS 33 Earnings per Share.explanation: If the number of ordinary or potential ordinary shares outstanding changes as a result of a capitalisation, bonus issue or share split, or a reverse share split, basic and diluted EPS for all periods presented are adjusted retrospectively.

options: Profit after tax divided by number of shares at year end

Profit divided by number of shares at the start of the period
Profit divided by weighted average number of ordinary shares
